content:
# Acts Chapter 16 -- The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus, PDF pages 245-246 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/ACT_016_page_001.jpg, ACT_016_page_002.jpg

---

**1** He-arrived and to Derbe and to Lystra. And behold, a certain disciple was there by-name Timothy, son of-a woman, a Jewish [woman] believing, but [the] father of-him [was] a Greek,

**2** who was-being-witnessed-to by the brothers in Lystra and Iconium.

**3** This-[one] Paul wished to-go-out with him, and having-taken [him] he-circumcised him on-account-of the Jews being in those places; for they-knew all that [the] father of-him was a Greek.

**4** And as they-were-passing-through the cities, they-were-delivering to-them the decrees to-keep, the-[ones] having-been-decided by the apostles and elders in Jerusalem.

**5** Indeed therefore the churches were-being-strengthened in-the faith and were-abounding in-number daily.

**6** And they-passed-through the Phrygian and Galatian region, having-been-hindered by the Holy Spirit to-speak the word in Asia.

**7** And having-come to Mysia, they-were-attempting to-go into Bithynia, and the Spirit of-Jesus did not permit them.

**8** And having-passed-by Mysia, they-went-down to Troas.

**9** And a vision through the night appeared to-Paul: a certain man, a Macedonian, was standing and entreating him and saying, "Having-crossed-over into Macedonia, help us."

**10** And when he-saw the vision, immediately we-sought to-go-forth into Macedonia, concluding that God has-called us to-proclaim-the-good-news to-them.

**11** And having-put-out-to-sea from Troas, we-ran-a-straight-course to Samothrace, and the next-[day] to Neapolis,

**12** and from-there to Philippi, which is a first city of-the district of-Macedonia, a colony. And we-were in this city remaining [for] some days.

**13** And on-the day of-the sabbaths we-went-out outside the gate beside a river where we-supposed prayer to-be, and having-sat-down we-were-speaking to-the women having-come-together.

**14** And a certain woman by-name Lydia, a seller-of-purple, of-[the] city of-Thyatira, worshipping God, was-hearing, whose heart the Lord opened to-give-heed to-the-[things] being-spoken by Paul.

**15** And when she-was-baptized and the household of-her, she-entreated saying, "If you-have-judged me to-be faithful to-the Lord, having-entered into the house of-me, remain." And she-constrained us.

**16** And it-came-to-pass, [while] going of-us to the prayer, a certain slave-girl having a spirit of-Python met us, who was-providing much gain to-the masters of-her by-divining.

**17** This-[one] having-followed Paul and us was-crying-out saying, "These men are servants of-the God Most-High, who are-proclaiming to-you a way of-salvation."

**18** And this she-was-doing for many days. But Paul, having-been-greatly-troubled and having-turned, said to-the spirit, "I-command you in [the] name of-Jesus Christ to-come-out from her." And it-came-out the same hour.

**19** And the masters of-her, having-seen that the hope of-the gain of-them went-out, having-seized Paul and Silas, dragged [them] into the marketplace before the rulers,

**20** and having-brought them to-the magistrates, said, "These men are-greatly-disturbing our city, being Jews,

**21** and are-proclaiming customs which it-is not lawful for-us to-accept nor to-do, being Romans."

**22** And the crowd rose-up-together against them, and the magistrates, having-torn-off the garments of-them, were-commanding to-beat-[them]-with-rods.

**23** Many and blows having-laid-on them, they-threw [them] into prison, having-charged the jailer to-keep them securely;

**24** who, such a charge having-received, threw them into the inner prison and fastened the feet of-them into the stocks.

**25** And around midnight Paul and Silas, praying, were-singing-hymns-to God, and the prisoners were-listening-to them.

**26** And suddenly a great earthquake came, so-as the foundations of-the prison to-be-shaken; and immediately all the doors were-opened, and the bonds of-all were-loosened.

**27** And the jailer, having-become awake and having-seen the doors of-the prison having-been-opened, having-drawn [his] sword, was-about to-kill himself, supposing the prisoners to-have-escaped.

**28** But Paul called-out [with] a great voice saying, "Do no harm to-yourself, for we-are all here."

**29** And having-asked-for lights, he-rushed-in, and trembling having-become, he-fell-before Paul and Silas,

**30** and having-led them outside, he-said, "Lords, what must I do that I-may-be-saved?"

**31** And they said, "Believe on the Lord Jesus, and you-shall-be-saved, you and the household of-you."

**32** And they-spoke to-him the word of-the Lord, together-with all the-[ones] in the house of-him.

**33** And having-taken them in that hour of-the night, he-washed [them] from the wounds, and was-baptized, he and all the-[ones] of-him, immediately.

**34** And having-brought them into the house, he-set-before [them] a table, and rejoiced with-all-[his]-household having-believed in-God.

**35** And day having-come, the magistrates sent the rod-bearers saying, "Release those men."

**36** And the jailer reported these words to Paul, that "The magistrates have-sent that you-may-be-released; now therefore having-gone-out, go in peace."

**37** But Paul said to them, "Having-beaten us publicly, uncondemned, men Romans being, they-threw [us] into prison; and now secretly they-are-casting us out? No indeed, but having-come themselves, let-them-lead us out."

**38** And the rod-bearers reported to-the magistrates these words; and they-feared, having-heard that they-are Romans.

**39** And having-come, they-entreated them, and having-led-[them]-out, they-were-asking [them] to-depart from the city.

**40** And having-gone-out from the prison, they-entered to Lydia, and having-seen the brothers, they-encouraged them and departed.

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

**Acts 16 -- Codex Sinaiticus Observations:**

1. **Verse 7 -- "Spirit of Jesus"**: Sinaiticus reads ΠΝΕΥΜΑ ΙΗΣΟΥ ("Spirit of Jesus") rather than simply "the Spirit" as found in some later manuscripts. This is a significant Christological reading preserved in the earliest witnesses.

2. **Verse 12 -- "first city of the district"**: Sinaiticus reads ΠΡΩΤΗ ΤΗΣ ΜΕΡΙΔΟΣ ("first of the district"), a disputed phrase. Some manuscripts read ΠΡΩΤΗΣ ΜΕΡΙΔΟΣ ("of a first district"). The grammar is awkward in all witnesses; the meaning likely conveys Philippi's prominence rather than strict administrative ranking.

3. **Verse 13 -- "where we supposed prayer to be"**: Sinaiticus reads ΕΝΟΜΙΖΟΜΕΝ ΠΡΟΣΕΥΧΗΝ ΕΙΝΑΙ ("we supposed prayer to be"), using the imperfect. Some witnesses read ΠΡΟΣΕΥΧΗ ("a place of prayer") rather than the verbal construction.

4. **Verse 16 -- "spirit of Python"**: Sinaiticus preserves ΠΝΕΥΜΑ ΠΥΘΩΝΑ ("spirit of Python"), referencing the Pythonic oracle spirit associated with Delphi. This is consistently rendered across major uncials.

5. **Verse 17 -- "a way of salvation"**: Sinaiticus reads ΟΔΟΝ ΣΩΤΗΡΙΑΣ ("a way of salvation") without the definite article, whereas some later manuscripts insert THE (ΤΗΝ), reading "the way of salvation." The anarthrous reading in Sinaiticus is noteworthy.

6. **Verse 31 -- "the Lord Jesus"**: Sinaiticus reads ΚΥΡΙΟΝ ΙΗΣΟΥΝ ("Lord Jesus") without ΧΡΙΣΤΟΝ ("Christ"). The Byzantine tradition typically expands to "the Lord Jesus Christ." The shorter reading is characteristic of the earlier Alexandrian text-type.

7. **Verse 32 -- "the word of the Lord"**: Sinaiticus reads ΤΟΝ ΛΟΓΟΝ ΤΟΥ ΚΥΡΙΟΥ ("the word of the Lord"), while some manuscripts substitute ΤΟΥ ΘΕΟΥ ("of God").

8. **Verse 35 -- "rod-bearers" (rhabdouchous)**: The Greek ΡΑΒΔΟΥΧΟΥΣ refers to the Roman lictors who carried the fasces (bundles of rods), the official attendants of Roman magistrates. This is a technical Greco-Roman administrative term.

9. **"We" passages**: Acts 16:10 marks the first appearance of the famous "we" narrative sections in Acts (vv. 10-17), where the author shifts from third person to first person plural, traditionally understood as indicating Luke's personal presence with Paul. Sinaiticus preserves this transition clearly.

10. **Verse 6 -- "Phrygian and Galatian region"**: Sinaiticus reads ΤΗΝ ΦΡΥΓΙΑΝ ΚΑΙ ΓΑΛΑΤΙΚΗΝ ΧΩΡΑΝ, treating "Phrygian and Galatian" as two adjectives modifying one region, rather than two separate regions. This has implications for the North vs. South Galatian hypothesis.

-- EMET
